After a goalless 90, La Roja broke Argentina's resistance in extra time — Ferran Torres, 106' — to seal Spain's second World Cup and a first since 2010.

The Match

A final decided in the dark

Spain went the entire tournament without conceding more than once, and in the final they didn't concede at all.

Emiliano Martínez stood tall for long stretches, denying chance after chance as Spain piled on 20 shots to Argentina's two. But nothing could hold forever. Thirty-nine seconds into the second period of extra time, substitute Ferran Torres met a low ball inside the box and finished coolly past the Argentine keeper — a strike that instantly entered Spanish football folklore.

It capped a suffocating defensive campaign: Spain conceded just once across all eight matches, the miserly foundation Rodri and company built the entire run on. Sixteen years after their first title in 2010, La Roja are champions of the world once more.

Ferran Torres · The Winner, 106'

Spain's Road to Glory

Eight matches, one goal conceded

01 Group Stage Opener Drew tournament debutants Cape Verde before finding their rhythm
02 Knockout Rounds Dominated possession, dictated tempo against elite opposition
03 Defensive Record Conceded just one goal in the entire campaign — a single strike, one match
04 The Final Outlasted Argentina 1–0 after extra time to lift a second World Cup

Respect Due

Argentina's campaign, and Messi's farewell

Argentina arrived chasing a fourth straight major title and left with nothing but respect earned the hard way. A second-half red card in the final made an already uphill night steeper, and for long stretches it was only Emiliano Martínez's goalkeeping keeping the scoreline level.

It is widely believed to be Lionel Messi's last World Cup appearance — a quiet ending to a career that reshaped the sport. Great champions are measured not only by the trophies they lift, but by how they compete when the night doesn't go their way, and Argentina did that until the final whistle.

Football doesn't ask where you're from. It only asks who you're cheering for.
Quote of the Tournament

CAMPEONES DEL MUNDO

Sixteen years after their first star, Spain add a second — built on a defence that gave almost nothing away, and a substitute who needed only 39 seconds to make history.
